sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=58, col=35).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 4, level 1.
Valid actions right now: SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The game has a target color pattern at top (blue, light green, yellow, purple), a red frame with 4 gray dots in center, and a color palette at bottom (light green, purple, blue, yellow). SPACE advances a timer bar. Need to figure out how to color the gray dots to match the top pattern.
- Plan: Try clicking on bottom colored squares to see if they "paint" the gray dots in the center. Start by clicking the blue square (3rd from left) since blue is the first color in the top pattern.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 3 of about 18 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I can see from the images that clicking on the blue square at the bottom caused it to get a white border (selected). This confirms that clicking bottom squares selects them. Now I need to figure out what happens next - maybe I need to click on the gray dots after selecting a color, or maybe SPACE applies the selected color.
Let me check what changed more precisely, then try clicking on the first gray dot to see if it gets colored blue.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking bottom colored squares selects them (white border appears). The blue square is now selected. Next I need to apply this color to the gray dots in the center, likely by clicking on them.
Plan: Click on the first gray dot (leftmost) to paint it blue, matching the first color in the top pattern.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Check what changed after clicking blue square
if last_transition and last_transition.before_frame:
    before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    
    before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before['nodes']}
    after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after['nodes']}
    
    print("Differences after clicking blue square:")
    all_ids = set(list(before_nodes.keys()) + list(after_nodes.keys()))
    for nid in sorted(all_ids):
        b = before_nodes.get(nid)
        a = after_nodes.get(nid)
        if b is None:
            print(f"  Node {nid} appeared: color={a['color']}, pixels={a['pixels']}")
        elif a is None:
            print(f"  Node {nid} disappeared: color={b['color']}, pixels={b['pixels']}")
        elif b['color'] != a['color'] or b['pixels'] != a['pixels'] or b['boundary'] != a['boundary']:
            print(f"  Node {nid} changed: color {b['color']}->{a['color']}, pixels {b['pixels']}->{a['pixels']}")

# The blue square got selected (white border). Now let's try clicking on the first gray dot.
# Gray dots are at approximately:
# Dot 1: [29,22]-[30,23] -> center at row 29, col 22
# Dot 2: [29,28]-[30,29] -> center at row 29, col 28
# Dot 3: [29,34]-[30,35] -> center at row 29, col 34
# Dot 4: [29,40]-[30,41] -> center at row 29, col 40

# Let me click on the first gray dot to see if it gets colored blue
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 29, 'col': 22}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Differences after clicking blue square:
  Node 22 changed: color c->c, pixels 576->556
  Node 23 changed: color N->W, pixels 16->20
  Node 24 changed: color p->N, pixels 16->16
  Node 25 changed: color b->p, pixels 16->16
  Node 26 changed: color Y->b, pixels 16->16
  Node 27 appeared: color=Y, pixels=16
